title: White soda bread
subtitle: Irish quick bread with no yeast
excerpt: A dense, tender Irish loaf leavened with bicarbonate of soda and buttermilk. No yeast, no proving, no kneading. Mixed in minutes and baked in forty. The crust is scored into four quarters in the traditional way.
sourceNotes: Darina Allen, Irish Traditional Cooking (1995) — the traditional Irish soda bread formula: plain flour, buttermilk, bicarbonate of soda, and salt, scored in four quarters. The method is unchanged from the nineteenth century; the original griddle is replaced here by the oven.

recipe.freezeNotes: Slice and freeze. Toast directly from frozen.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Best eaten the day it is made. Keeps for 2 days wrapped in a clean cloth.

glossaryTerms.definition: A slightly sour, thickened dairy liquid. In modern production it is skimmed milk cultured with lactic bacteria; traditionally it was the liquid left after churning cream into butter. Its acidity reacts with bicarbonate of soda to produce carbon dioxide, which leavens soda bread without yeast.

content.text: Soda bread works because bicarbonate of soda reacts with the acid in

to produce carbon dioxide. No yeast, no kneading, no proving. The dough goes from bowl to oven in under ten minutes, and the cross scored on top is not decorative — it lets steam escape and helps the loaf bake through to the centre.
Heat the oven to 200°C fan. Dust a baking tray with flour.

Combine the {{plain-flour}}, {{bicarbonate-of-soda}}, and {{fine-sea-salt}} in a large bowl. Make a well in the centre and pour in the {{buttermilk}}. Stir with a fork until a rough, shaggy dough forms. Do not overmix — a few seconds of stirring is enough. Overworking develops gluten and makes the loaf tough.

Turn the dough onto a lightly floured surface and shape it quickly into a round about 4 cm tall. Do not knead. Place on the prepared tray and cut a deep cross through the top, going almost to the base.

Bake for 35–40 minutes until the loaf is deep golden and sounds hollow when tapped firmly on the base. Transfer to a rack and leave for at least 15 minutes before slicing. Cutting into hot soda bread compresses the crumb.
